title: On Minkowski's Monotonicity Problem
speaker: Ramon van Handel
abstract: More than 120 years ago, Minkowski published a seminal paper that laid the foundation for the field of convex geometry (as well as several other areas of mathematics). Despite numerous advances in the intervening years, there are fundamental questions that arise in Minkowski's original work that remain open to this day. Major progress on one of these questions, the characterization of the extremals of the Alexandrov-Fenchel inequality, was achieved only in the past few years in joint work with Yair Shenfeld. In this talk I aim to discuss partial progress, in joint work with Shouda Wang, on another open problem that dates back to the same work: the equality characterization for the monotonicity of mixed volumes. No prior background on convex geometry will be assumed; instead I will aim to explain the problem, from a geometric analysis perspective, as a mixed analogue of the classical fact (which dates back to Euler) that a surface with vanishing Gaussian curvature is a ruled surface.

proof_stages.label: Mixed volumes and Minkowski monotonicity
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines mixed volumes as coefficients of the volume polynomial for Minkowski sums, recalls Alexandrov-Fenchel and monotonicity, and poses Minkowski's problem of characterizing equality in monotonicity.

proof_stages.label: Support-function and mixed-area-measure reformulation
proof_stages.summary: Support functions turn Minkowski addition into addition of functions, allowing mixed volume in one slot to be represented by integration against a positive mixed area measure, so equality for K subset L is equivalent to equality of support functions on the support of that measure.

proof_stages.label: One-body smooth case and Monge-Ampere degeneracy
proof_stages.summary: For one convex body with C^2 support function, the mixed area measure has density det(Hess h), so being off the support is the homogeneous Monge-Ampere equation, interpreted via a local-to-global ruled-line/normal-cone picture.

proof_stages.label: Schneider's arbitrary one-body support theorem
proof_stages.summary: Schneider's theorem extends the one-body picture to all convex bodies by identifying the support of S(C,...,C) with the closure of directions whose normal cone is one-dimensional.

proof_stages.label: Polarization to the smooth mixed case
proof_stages.summary: For several bodies with C^2 support functions, polarizing the determinant gives the mixed discriminant, and the mixed area measure has density equal to the mixed discriminant of the Hessians.

proof_stages.label: Linear-algebra criterion and conjectural support formula
proof_stages.summary: The nonvanishing criterion for mixed discriminants of positive semidefinite matrices is translated into a conjecture that the support consists, up to closure, of directions admitting linearly independent vectors orthogonal to the corresponding normal cones of the bodies.

proof_stages.label: Partial results, known cases, and obstruction
proof_stages.summary: The speaker reports that one inclusion of the conjecture is proved in general, the opposite inclusion is known in dimension three and special classes such as polytopes and zonoids, while the higher-dimensional and even C^2 cases remain open.

examples.name: Two line segments in the plane
examples.note: Two one-dimensional convex bodies in R^2 can have a sum with positive area, illustrating that mixed volumes need not vanish merely because the individual bodies are lower-dimensional.

examples.name: Surface area versus mean width in R^3

examples.name: Ruled surfaces and folded paper
examples.note: Surfaces of vanishing Gaussian curvature, pictured as a folded sheet of paper ruled by straight lines, make concrete the local-to-global phenomenon behind the homogeneous Monge-Ampere equation.
examples.engagement_reason: he reasons through ruled surfaces via the folded-paper picture and connects vanishing Hessian determinant/Gauss curvature to foliations by straight lines
